walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Alien Frontiers set up. Unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. The board shows Planet Maxwell at the bottom with its eight named territories, and orbital facilities arranged around the edges of space above the planet. Each facility has a specific number of docking ports where you will place your dice during the game. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Place the Scoring Track Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching scoring token. Place all scoring tokens on the zero space of the scoring track that runs around the edge of the board. You will move these forward as you place colonies and gain territory control throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Territory Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the eight territory tiles. Each one corresponds to a named territory on Planet Maxwell. Shuffle them face down and place one randomly on each territory space on the board. These tiles determine the special bonus that the controlling player of each territory receives. Flip them face up once placed so everyone can see the bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Stocks
walkthrough.text: Separate the resource tokens into two piles. Orange tokens are Fuel and gray tokens are Ore. Place both piles near the board where everyone can reach them. These form the shared supply. During the game, you can never hold more than eight total resource tokens at any time, so you will be spending them frequently.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Alien Tech Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all of the Alien Tech cards into a single face-down deck and place it near the board. Then deal three cards face up next to the deck. These three face-up cards form the Alien Tech display. Players can acquire these cards by using the Alien Artifact facility, and new cards are drawn to refill the display whenever one is taken.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Field Generators
walkthrough.text: Find the three field generator tokens: Isolation Field, Positron Field, and Repulsor Field. Place them in a shared area near the board. These can be placed on territories during the game to modify their properties. The Isolation Field nullifies a territory bonus, the Positron Field adds one victory point for the controller, and the Repulsor Field prevents colonies from being added or removed.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Ships and Colonies
walkthrough.text: Each player takes three dice of their chosen color. These are your ships. Place all three ships in the Maintenance Bay area of the board to start. Each player also takes six colony tokens of their matching color and keeps them in front of them. In a two-player game, each player uses six colonies. In a three-player game, use six each. In a four-player game, use six each as well. You win by being the first to place all of your colonies on the planet.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Alien Tech Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal one Alien Tech card face down to each player from the top of the deck. Players may look at their own card but should keep it hidden from opponents. This gives everyone a starting technology to work with from the very first turn.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Starting resources depend on turn order. The first player receives one Fuel. The second player receives one Fuel and one Ore. The third player receives two Fuel and one Ore. The fourth player receives two Fuel and two Ore. Later players get more resources to compensate for going later. Place your resources in front of you where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Everyone rolls all three of their dice. The player with the highest total goes first. If there is a tie, the tied players reroll. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. Once the first player is determined, distribute starting resources according to turn order as described in the previous step. You are ready to play Alien Frontiers.

Keep your card hidden from opponents

2nd player: 1 Fuel + 1 Ore

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: All players roll their 3 dice — highest total goes first
Ties reroll, play proceeds clockwise
Distribute starting resources by turn order

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do three things in order. First, gather all of your ships from the board back into your hand. Every die you placed on orbital facilities last turn comes back to you. Second, roll all of your dice. These rolls determine which facilities you can use this turn. Third, assign your ships to orbital facilities on the board. You must dock all of your ships if possible. Any ships that genuinely cannot dock anywhere go to the Maintenance Bay, which provides no benefit. Let's walk through each orbital facility so you know your options.

walkthrough.title: Solar Converter
walkthrough.text: The Solar Converter is the simplest facility and your primary source of Fuel. It has eight docking ports and accepts any die value, so you can always dock here. For each ship you place, you gain Fuel equal to half the die value, rounded up. So a die showing one or two gives you one Fuel. A three or four gives you two Fuel. A five or six gives you three Fuel. This is reliable income and a good place for dice that do not fit anywhere better.

walkthrough.title: Lunar Mine
walkthrough.text: The Lunar Mine is your main source of Ore. It has five docking ports, but there is a key restriction: each new ship you dock here must show a value equal to or higher than the highest die already docked at the Lunar Mine by any player. So if someone has already placed a four here, you need a four, five, or six. Each ship you dock earns you one Ore. The escalating value requirement means early access is easier, and it gets harder as the turn order progresses.

walkthrough.title: Alien Artifact
walkthrough.text: The Alien Artifact facility has four docking ports and accepts any die value. It offers two different actions depending on how many ships you dock. If you place any number of ships here, you may cycle the Alien Tech display: discard all three face-up cards and deal three new ones from the deck. If your ships docked here have a combined total of eight or more, you may claim one face-up Alien Tech card from the display instead. You can cycle first and then claim if your total is high enough, giving you a fresh selection.

walkthrough.title: Colonist Hub
walkthrough.text: The Colonist Hub is the slow but steady path to placing colonies. It has four parallel tracks, each with three docking ports and a seven-circle advancement track. On your turn, you dock one ship per track. Your colony token advances along the circles based on the die value: a higher roll moves you further. When your colony reaches the end of a track after multiple turns of advancement, you launch it onto a territory by paying one Fuel and one Ore. This is the most reliable colonization method because it does not require specific die combinations.

walkthrough.title: Colony Constructor
walkthrough.text: The Colony Constructor lets you place a colony immediately, but it requires a specific dice combination. You need three ships all showing the same value, plus three Ore. Dock the three matching dice and pay the Ore, then place one of your colony tokens on any territory of your choice. This is expensive but fast. No waiting, no advancement tracks. If you can roll triples and have the Ore stockpiled, this is the quickest way to colonize.

walkthrough.title: Terraforming Station
walkthrough.text: The Terraforming Station is another way to place a colony immediately, and it is cheaper than the Colony Constructor. You need one ship showing a six, plus one Fuel and one Ore. Dock the die, pay the resources, and place a colony. Here is the catch: the ship you dock here is consumed. It goes back to the general stock, not to your supply. You get it back at the start of your next turn, but during the intervening round you have one fewer die to work with. This is a powerful facility for rushing colonies, but losing a ship temporarily is a real cost.

walkthrough.title: Shipyard
walkthrough.text: The Shipyard is where you build new ships to expand your fleet beyond the starting three. You need two ships showing the same value, plus a combination of Fuel and Ore. Building your fourth ship costs one Fuel and one Ore. Your fifth ship costs two Fuel and two Ore. Your sixth ship costs three Fuel and three Ore. More dice means more options every turn, and expanding your fleet early is one of the strongest strategies in the game. You can have a maximum of six ships.

walkthrough.title: Raiders' Outpost
walkthrough.text: The Raiders' Outpost is the game's primary interaction facility. You need three ships showing sequential values, like one-two-three, two-three-four, three-four-five, or four-five-six. Dock all three dice here to steal from opponents. You can either steal four resource tokens total from any combination of opponents, or steal one Alien Tech card from a single opponent. If another player later docks a higher sequence at the Raiders' Outpost, they bump your ships out and take over. This is a powerful but risky facility because your ships can be displaced.

walkthrough.title: Orbital Market
walkthrough.text: The Orbital Market lets you convert Fuel into Ore. You need two ships showing the same value. Dock them, then pay Fuel equal to the die value to gain one Ore. So two threes cost three Fuel for one Ore. Two ones cost only one Fuel for one Ore, which is the best exchange rate. You can make multiple transactions in a single docking, paying the Fuel cost each time. This is useful when you have plenty of Fuel from the Solar Converter but need Ore for building.

walkthrough.title: Territory Control and Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Planet Maxwell has eight territories. Whoever has the most colonies in a territory controls it and scores one bonus victory point. If players are tied for most colonies, no one controls that territory. Each territory also has a special bonus tile that grants the controlling player a unique advantage. For example, Lem Badlands gives plus one Fuel per ship at the Solar Converter. Herbert Valley reduces the cost of Shipyard builds by one Fuel and one Ore. These bonuses are powerful and can shape your strategy. Territory control is checked constantly, so it can shift whenever anyone places or loses a colony.

walkthrough.title: Alien Tech Cards
walkthrough.text: Alien Tech cards give you special powers that can bend or break the normal rules. Each card has two abilities: one that costs Fuel to activate, and one that requires discarding the card permanently. You can only use one discard ability per turn. Some examples: the Gravity Manipulator lets you adjust a die by plus or minus one. The Orbital Teleporter lets you move a colony from one territory to another. The Plasma Cannon lets you destroy an opponent's colony. These cards add a layer of tactical depth and surprise to the game.

walkthrough.title: Resource Cap and Maintenance Bay
walkthrough.text: Two important housekeeping rules. First, you can never hold more than eight resource tokens total. Fuel and Ore combined cannot exceed eight. If you would gain resources beyond the cap, you simply do not take the excess. Plan your spending to avoid wasting potential income. Second, the Maintenance Bay is where ships go when they genuinely cannot dock anywhere else. Ships in the Maintenance Bay provide no benefit. You should always try to dock all your ships at real facilities before resorting to the Maintenance Bay.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player places their last colony token onto the planet. That player wins. If you want to verify scores, count up each player's victory points: one point per colony placed on the planet, plus one point per territory controlled, plus any bonus points from the Positron Field generator. The player who placed their last colony should have the highest score. In the rare case of a tie, the player who triggered the game end wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Planet Maxwell
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through scripted turns for three players — Red, Blue, and Green — to show how a real game of Alien Frontiers flows. Each player starts with three dice in the Maintenance Bay and six colony tokens. Red goes first with one Fuel, Blue goes second with one Fuel and one Ore, and Green goes third with two Fuel and one Ore. Three Alien Tech cards are face-up in the display, and each player has one secret Alien Tech card. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn 1: Gather and Roll
walkthrough.text: Red picks up all three dice from the Maintenance Bay and rolls them. The results are 2, 4, and 5. Now Red needs to assign all three ships to orbital facilities. With only one Fuel and no Ore, Red's options are limited, but the Solar Converter and Lunar Mine are wide open since no one has docked yet this round.

walkthrough.title: Red Docks at the Solar Converter
walkthrough.text: Red places the die showing 4 at the Solar Converter. Half of four, rounded up, is two. Red gains two Fuel from the supply, bringing Red's total to three Fuel. The Solar Converter accepts any value and has eight ports, so it is always available. This is a solid opening move to build up resources.

walkthrough.title: Red Docks at the Lunar Mine
walkthrough.text: Red places the die showing 5 at the Lunar Mine. Since no other ships are docked there yet, any value works. Red gains one Ore. Now Red has three Fuel and one Ore. Red places the remaining die showing 2 at the Lunar Mine as well. Wait — the highest die there is now 5, and 2 is less than 5. Red cannot dock a lower value after a higher one.

walkthrough.title: Correcting Red's Mistake
walkthrough.text: Good catch. Red should have docked the lower die at the Lunar Mine first. Let's rewind. Red docks the 2 at the Lunar Mine first, gaining one Ore. Then Red docks the 5 at the Lunar Mine, since 5 is greater than 2. Red gains another Ore. Now Red has three Fuel and two Ore. The lesson: when using the Lunar Mine with multiple ships, always dock the lowest value first and work your way up.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Turn 1: Gather and Roll
walkthrough.text: Blue picks up three dice and rolls: 1, 1, and 6. Interesting roll. Blue starts with one Fuel and one Ore. Those double ones could go to the Orbital Market for a cheap Fuel-to-Ore conversion, or Blue could use the 6 at the Terraforming Station. But placing a colony on turn one would consume the die and leave Blue with only two ships next turn. Let's see what Blue decides.

walkthrough.title: Blue Plans for the Shipyard
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to build a fourth ship instead. Blue docks the two 1s at the Shipyard and pays one Fuel and one Ore, which is the cost for a fourth ship. Blue takes a new die from the supply and adds it to their fleet. Blue now has four ships for next turn. Blue still needs to dock the 6 somewhere. Blue places it at the Solar Converter and gains three Fuel. Blue ends the turn with three Fuel, zero Ore, and four ships. Great opening.

walkthrough.title: Green's Turn 1: Gather and Roll
walkthrough.text: Green picks up three dice and rolls: 3, 3, and 3. Triple threes. Green starts with two Fuel and one Ore. Green considers the options. Three matching dice could go to the Colony Constructor, but that requires three Ore and Green only has one. Green could also use the doubles at the Shipyard like Blue did.

walkthrough.title: Green Builds at the Shipyard
walkthrough.text: Green docks two of the threes at the Shipyard and pays one Fuel and one Ore for a fourth ship. Nice. Green places the remaining 3 at the Solar Converter, gaining two Fuel. Green ends the turn with three Fuel, zero Ore, and four ships. Both Blue and Green prioritized fleet expansion on turn one. This is a strong opening strategy because more dice means more options every single turn going forward.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn 2: Gather and Roll
walkthrough.text: Red gathers all three dice and rolls: 3, 3, and 5. Red has three Fuel and two Ore from last turn. Red notices that Blue and Green both have four ships now while Red still has three. Red should probably build a ship too. The double threes can go to the Shipyard.

walkthrough.title: Red Builds a Ship and Mines Ore
walkthrough.text: Red docks the two 3s at the Shipyard and pays one Fuel and one Ore. Red gains a fourth die. Now everyone has four ships. Red places the 5 at the Lunar Mine. The Lunar Mine is currently empty since all ships were gathered at the start of the turn, so any value works. Red gains one Ore. Red ends with two Fuel and two Ore.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Turn 2: Gather and Roll
walkthrough.text: Blue gathers four dice and rolls: 2, 3, 4, and 6. Four dice gives Blue many more options than last turn. That 2-3-4 is a sequential run, which means Blue could dock at the Raiders' Outpost to steal resources. But is it too early to raid? Let's see.

walkthrough.title: Blue Raids and Collects
walkthrough.text: Blue docks the 2, 3, and 4 at the Raiders' Outpost and steals four resources from opponents. Blue takes two Fuel from Red and two Ore from Green. Ouch. Blue places the remaining 6 at the Solar Converter for three more Fuel. Blue now has six Fuel and two Ore. Raiding early is aggressive but effective when opponents have stockpiled resources. Red and Green will remember this.

walkthrough.title: Green's Turn 2: Revenge on the Raiders
walkthrough.text: Green gathers four dice and rolls: 3, 4, 5, and 6. Green has a 3-4-5 run and a 4-5-6 run. The 4-5-6 is a higher sequence than Blue's 2-3-4 currently at the Raiders' Outpost. If Green docks a higher sequence, it bumps Blue's ships out. Green docks the 4, 5, and 6 at the Raiders' Outpost, bumping Blue's ships to the Maintenance Bay. Green steals four resources from Blue. Turnabout is fair play.

walkthrough.title: Green's Remaining Ship
walkthrough.text: Green still has the die showing 3 to place. Green docks it at the Colonist Hub on an empty track, advancing a colony token along the track. The Colonist Hub is slow — it takes multiple turns to advance a colony to the launch point — but it works with any die value and does not require specific combinations. Green is playing the long game with this colony while using raids for short-term resource advantage.

walkthrough.title: Red Tries the Wrong Approach
walkthrough.text: Let's look at a common mistake. Imagine Red rolls 4, 4, and 5 on a later turn and wants to build a fifth ship at the Shipyard. Red docks the two 4s there and tries to pay one Fuel and one Ore. But wait — that is the cost for a fourth ship, and Red already has four. The fifth ship costs two Fuel and two Ore. Always check the ship cost table: fourth ship is 1/1, fifth is 2/2, sixth is 3/3. The cost increases with each new ship.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Territory Control
walkthrough.text: Let's say after a few more turns, Red places a colony on Lem Badlands and Green places a colony on Lem Badlands. They are tied with one colony each, so no one controls the territory and no one gets the bonus victory point or the territory's special ability. If Red later places a second colony there, Red takes control, gains one VP, and gets the Lem Badlands bonus: plus one Fuel per ship docked at the Solar Converter. Territory control is a constant tug-of-war.

walkthrough.title: Using Alien Tech Cards
walkthrough.text: Green has been holding a Gravity Manipulator card. This card lets Green pay one Fuel to adjust any one die roll by plus or minus one. Green rolls a 5 and needs a 6 for the Terraforming Station. Green pays one Fuel to activate the Gravity Manipulator and bumps the 5 up to a 6. Now Green can use the Terraforming Station to place a colony for just one additional Fuel and one Ore. Alien Tech cards are game-changers when used at the right moment.

walkthrough.title: The Terraforming Station Trade-Off
walkthrough.text: Green docks the adjusted die at the Terraforming Station, pays one Fuel and one Ore, and places a colony. But remember — the ship is consumed. That die goes back to the general stock, and Green will not have it until the start of their next turn. So Green temporarily drops from four ships to three. This is a real cost. Is placing a colony one turn earlier worth having fewer options for a round? Sometimes yes, especially in the endgame race.

walkthrough.title: The Colony Constructor Alternative
walkthrough.text: Compare that to the Colony Constructor. If Green had rolled triple 4s and had three Ore stockpiled, Green could dock all three matching dice and pay three Ore to place a colony. No ship is consumed — all three dice come back next turn. The Colony Constructor is more expensive in resources and requires a lucky roll, but you keep your full fleet. Both paths have trade-offs, and knowing when to use each one is a key skill.

walkthrough.title: Watching the Colony Count
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, keep a close eye on how many colonies each player has placed. The game ends the instant someone places their last colony. If Red has placed five of six colonies and has resources stockpiled, Red is one colony away from winning. Blue and Green need to either race to finish first or use disruptive plays — stealing Red's resources with the Raiders' Outpost, placing an Isolation Field on Red's key territory, or using a Plasma Cannon card to destroy one of Red's colonies. Awareness of opponents' colony counts is critical.

walkthrough.title: Field Generators in Action
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to place a Repulsor Field on Lem Badlands, where Red has two colonies and controls the territory. The Repulsor Field prevents any colonies from being added to or removed from that territory. This locks Red's control in place, but also prevents Red from adding more colonies there. Meanwhile, Blue places a Positron Field on a territory Blue controls, gaining one extra victory point. Field generators add a strategic layer beyond just placing colonies.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: The race is on. Red has five colonies placed, Blue has four, and Green has four. Red needs just one more colony to win. Red rolls dice and gets a 6 — perfect for the Terraforming Station. Red pays one Fuel and one Ore, sacrifices the ship, and places the sixth and final colony. The game ends immediately. Red counts up: six colonies for six points, plus control of two territories for two more points, totaling eight victory points. Red wins Alien Frontiers.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the core flow of Alien Frontiers in action. You watched players gather and roll dice, dock ships at orbital facilities, build new ships to expand their fleet, collect resources, raid opponents, use Alien Tech cards to bend the rules, and race to place all their colonies on Planet Maxwell. You learned the key mistakes to avoid: docking Lunar Mine ships in the wrong order, forgetting the escalating Shipyard costs, and ignoring opponents' colony counts. You are ready to colonize Planet Maxwell for real.

summary.title: Welcome to Planet Maxwell
bullets: 3 players: Red, Blue, Green — each starts with 3 dice and 6 colonies
Starting resources vary by turn order
3 face-up Alien Tech cards + 1 secret card each

bullets: Pick up all dice from Maintenance Bay
Roll: 2, 4, 5 — now assign to orbital facilities
Limited resources (1 Fuel) means focusing on income

Half of 4 rounded up = 2
Accepts any value, always available

Problem: 2 is less than 5 — violates escalating rule
Each new ship must be >= highest already docked

summary.title: Correcting Red's Mistake
bullets: Dock lowest value first (2), then higher (5)
Gain 1 Ore per ship = 2 Ore total
Lesson: always dock Lunar Mine ships low to high

summary.title: Blue's Turn 1: Gather and Roll
bullets: Roll: 1, 1, 6 — double 1s are useful for Shipyard or Market
6 could go to Terraforming Station but consuming a ship on turn 1 is risky

summary.title: Blue Plans for the Shipyard

6 at Solar Converter = 3 Fuel
Blue ends with 4 ships — strong opening

summary.title: Green's Turn 1: Gather and Roll
bullets: Roll: 3, 3, 3 — triple threes
Colony Constructor needs 3 Ore (Green has 1) — can't afford
Shipyard is the better play

summary.title: Green Builds at the Shipyard
bullets: Two 3s at Shipyard + 1 Fuel + 1 Ore = 4th ship
Remaining 3 at Solar Converter = 2 Fuel
Both Blue and Green prioritized fleet expansion — smart

summary.title: Red's Turn 2: Gather and Roll
bullets: Roll: 3, 3, 5 with 3 Fuel and 2 Ore in stock
Red notices opponents have 4 ships — time to catch up
Double 3s can go to Shipyard

summary.title: Red Builds a Ship and Mines Ore

5 at Lunar Mine = 1 Ore
All players now have 4 ships

summary.title: Blue's Turn 2: Gather and Roll
bullets: Roll: 2, 3, 4, 6 — four dice, many options
2-3-4 is a sequential run for Raiders' Outpost
Aggressive but effective early

summary.title: Blue Raids and Collects
bullets: 2-3-4 at Raiders' Outpost → steal 4 resources from opponents
6 at Solar Converter → 3 Fuel
Blue ends with 6 Fuel, 2 Ore — resource rich

summary.title: Green's Turn 2: Revenge on the Raiders
bullets: Roll: 3, 4, 5, 6 — has 4-5-6 run (higher than Blue's 2-3-4)
Higher sequence bumps lower — Blue's ships go to Maintenance Bay
Green steals 4 resources from Blue

summary.title: Green's Remaining Ship
bullets: Die showing 3 goes to Colonist Hub — any value works
Colony advances along track toward launch point
Slow but reliable colonization path

summary.title: Red Tries the Wrong Approach
bullets: Red tries to pay 1/1 for a 5th ship — that's the 4th ship cost
5th ship costs 2 Fuel + 2 Ore, 6th costs 3+3
Lesson: always check the ship cost table

summary.title: Understanding Territory Control
bullets: Tied colonies = no controller, no VP, no bonus
Break the tie to gain control, 1 VP, and territory bonus
Territory control shifts dynamically throughout the game

summary.title: Using Alien Tech Cards
bullets: Gravity Manipulator: pay 1 Fuel to adjust a die by +/- 1
Green bumps a 5 to a 6 for the Terraforming Station
Right timing with Alien Tech cards can be game-changing

summary.title: The Terraforming Station Trade-Off
bullets: Place colony for 1 Fuel + 1 Ore, but ship is consumed
Temporarily lose 1 die until next turn
Worth it in endgame, risky in early game

summary.title: The Colony Constructor Alternative
bullets: 3 matching dice + 3 Ore = place colony, keep all ships
More expensive but no ship loss
Both methods have trade-offs — learn when to use each

summary.title: Watching the Colony Count
bullets: Game ends instantly when someone places their last colony
Track opponents' colony counts constantly
Use raids, fields, and Alien Tech to disrupt leaders

summary.title: Field Generators in Action
bullets: Repulsor Field: locks a territory — no colonies added or removed
Positron Field: +1 VP for territory controller
Isolation Field: nullifies territory bonus

summary.title: The Endgame Push
bullets: Red rolls a 6 → Terraforming Station → places 6th colony
Game ends immediately — Red wins
Final score: 6 colonies + 2 territories = 8 VP

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've seen dice placement, fleet building, raiding, and Alien Tech in action
Key mistakes: Lunar Mine order, Shipyard costs, ignoring colony counts
Check out General Tips and the Appendix for deeper strategy
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Expand Your Fleet Early
walkthrough.text: The single most important thing you can do in the opening turns is build more ships. You start with only three dice, and every extra die dramatically increases your options each turn. With four dice, you have access to the Shipyard, Raiders' Outpost, and Colony Constructor far more reliably. With five or six dice, you can do multiple powerful actions every turn. Prioritize building your fourth ship on turn one or two, even if it means skipping resource collection. The investment pays for itself almost immediately.

walkthrough.title: Don't Neglect Territory Control
walkthrough.text: New players tend to focus entirely on placing colonies and forget about where they place them. Territory control matters for two reasons. First, each controlled territory gives you one victory point, and in a game that often ends with scores in the single digits, one VP is significant. Second, territory bonuses can be extremely powerful. Controlling Herbert Valley saves you resources on every Shipyard build. Controlling Lem Badlands gives you extra Fuel every turn. Think about which territories benefit your strategy and colonize them deliberately.

walkthrough.title: The Colonist Hub Is Slow but Reliable
walkthrough.text: The Colonist Hub does not require matching dice, sequential dice, or a six. Any single die can advance your colony along the track. This makes it the most reliable colonization method, especially when your other rolls do not line up for the Colony Constructor or Terraforming Station. Start a Colonist Hub track early in the game, because it takes several turns to advance a colony to the launch point. Treat it as background progress that pays off later.

walkthrough.title: Raiders' Outpost Is Powerful but Situational
walkthrough.text: Stealing four resources or an Alien Tech card is a huge swing, but the Raiders' Outpost requires three sequential dice, which you will not always roll. Do not plan your entire strategy around raiding. Use it opportunistically when you happen to roll a run. Also remember that a higher sequence bumps a lower one, so your raiders can be displaced before your next turn. The best time to raid is when opponents have large resource stockpiles or a critical Alien Tech card you need.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Eight-Token Resource Cap
walkthrough.text: You can never hold more than eight resource tokens total. This means stockpiling resources without spending them is wasteful — you will hit the cap and lose potential income. Plan to spend resources the same turn you collect them, or at least the following turn. If you are heading into a big collection turn at the Solar Converter, make sure you have room in your supply. Conversely, if you are sitting at six or seven tokens, consider spending before collecting.

walkthrough.title: Alien Tech Cards Are Game-Changers
walkthrough.text: Do not underestimate Alien Tech cards. A well-timed Gravity Manipulator turns a bad roll into a perfect one. A Plasma Cannon can destroy an opponent's colony when they are about to win. An Orbital Teleporter can move your colony to snatch territory control at a critical moment. Always try to keep at least one Alien Tech card in reserve for the endgame. Use the Alien Artifact facility early to cycle through the display and find cards that complement your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Watch Opponents' Colony Counts
walkthrough.text: The game ends instantly when someone places their last colony. You need to track not just your own progress but everyone else's. If an opponent has five colonies placed and the resources to place a sixth, the game could end on their next turn. When someone is close to winning, shift your strategy to disruption: raid their resources, place fields on their territories, or use Alien Tech to slow them down. Being one turn too late to react is the most common way to lose.

walkthrough.title: The Orbital Market Is Better Than It Looks
walkthrough.text: New players often overlook the Orbital Market because paying Fuel for Ore seems like a bad trade. But the Solar Converter generates Fuel so efficiently that you will often have a surplus. Converting that surplus into Ore at the Market keeps your resources balanced. The exchange rate depends on your dice: two 1s means you pay just one Fuel per Ore, which is excellent. Two 6s means six Fuel per Ore, which is terrible. When you roll low doubles that cannot do anything else useful, the Orbital Market is a strong option.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Colonization Methods
walkthrough.text: Do not rely on a single method to place all your colonies. The Colonist Hub is reliable but slow. The Colony Constructor is fast but requires triples and lots of Ore. The Terraforming Station is cheap but costs you a ship for a round. Use a mix of all three based on your dice rolls and resource situation each turn. Flexibility is the key to consistent colony placement. If you tunnel on one method, a few bad rolls can stall you completely while opponents race ahead.

bullets: Build your 4th ship on turn 1 or 2 — top priority
More dice = exponentially more options each turn
Even skipping resource collection is worth it for early fleet expansion

Territory bonuses can be game-defining (Herbert Valley, Lem Badlands)
Place colonies deliberately, not randomly

Higher sequences bump lower ones — your raid can be displaced

Spend resources the same turn or next turn
Make room before big collection turns

Keep at least 1 card in reserve for the endgame

Low doubles (1-1) give the best exchange rate
Great use for low doubles that can't do anything else

summary.title: Diversify Your Colonization Methods

Each method has different dice/resource requirements
Flexibility prevents bad rolls from stalling you completely

walkthrough.title: The Shipyard Rush
walkthrough.text: The strongest opening in Alien Frontiers is building ships as fast as possible. On turn one, any pair of dice can go to the Shipyard for your fourth ship at a cost of just one Fuel and one Ore. On turn two, with four dice, you are much more likely to roll another pair for your fifth ship. Five dice is the sweet spot — you can reliably assign ships to multiple facilities every turn, and you have enough dice for the Raiders' Outpost, Colony Constructor, and other combo-heavy facilities. Some aggressive players push for six dice, but the cost of three Fuel and three Ore is steep and the sixth die only marginally improves your flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Territory Synergies
walkthrough.text: Certain territory bonuses synergize powerfully with specific strategies. Herbert Valley reduces Shipyard costs by one Fuel and one Ore, making fleet expansion almost free. If you are rushing ships, controlling Herbert Valley early is a massive advantage. Lem Badlands gives plus one Fuel per Solar Converter ship, which pairs with an Ore-hungry strategy — you generate extra Fuel to trade at the Orbital Market. Bradbury Plateau reduces Colony Constructor cost by one Ore, making triple-dice colonies cost only two Ore instead of three. Identify which territories support your plan and prioritize colonizing them.

walkthrough.title: Raiders' Timing and Target Selection
walkthrough.text: The Raiders' Outpost is most effective in the mid-game when opponents have built up resources but have not spent them yet. Stealing four resources from a player who just loaded up at the Solar Converter and Lunar Mine is devastating. Target the player who is closest to winning — disruption is more valuable than raw resource gain. Stealing an Alien Tech card can be even better than resources if the opponent has a powerful card like the Gravity Manipulator or Plasma Cannon. Remember that your raid can be bumped by a higher sequence, so raid early in the round when fewer players have taken their turns.

walkthrough.title: Colony Constructor vs. Terraforming Station
walkthrough.text: Both facilities place colonies immediately, but they serve different strategic roles. The Colony Constructor costs three matching dice and three Ore but preserves your fleet. Use it when you have a strong Ore economy and are rolling lots of triples. The Terraforming Station costs one six, one Fuel, and one Ore, but consumes a ship for a round. Use it in the endgame when you are racing to place your last colony and the temporary ship loss does not matter. In the mid-game, prefer the Colony Constructor because losing a ship for a round significantly reduces your options.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Opponents' Facilities
walkthrough.text: Orbital facilities have limited docking ports, and filling them up denies other players access. The Lunar Mine has only five ports and the escalating value requirement makes it easy to block with high dice. If you dock a 5 and a 6 at the Lunar Mine, only sixes can follow. The Colonist Hub tracks fill up too — if an opponent is advancing a colony on a track and you dock there first, you can slow their progress. The Alien Artifact has only four ports, so docking there can prevent opponents from acquiring key tech cards. Always consider whether docking at a facility helps you and hurts an opponent simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Alien Tech Combos
walkthrough.text: Certain Alien Tech cards combine powerfully. The Gravity Manipulator lets you adjust a die by plus or minus one, which can turn a near-miss into a perfect roll for the Terraforming Station, Colony Constructor, or Raiders' Outpost. The Data Crystal lets you place a ship at a facility that is already full, bypassing port limits. Combined with knowledge of which facilities matter most, this lets you break through blockades. The Temporal Warper lets you take another turn after your current one, which in the late game can mean placing two colonies before anyone can respond. Look for two-card synergies that multiply your options.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to shift from building to finishing is the most important skill in Alien Frontiers. The transition typically happens when you have placed four of your six colonies. At that point, you need just two more, and every turn should be focused on placing colonies rather than accumulating resources or ships. The Terraforming Station becomes your best friend in the endgame because it only requires one specific die and minimal resources. If you have five colonies placed, consider using Alien Tech discard powers aggressively — there is no point saving cards for a future that will not come. Pull the trigger before opponents can react.

walkthrough.title: Field Generator Placement
walkthrough.text: Field generators are strategic tools that most players use reactively, but the best players use them proactively. Place a Positron Field on a territory you control early, before opponents can contest it, to lock in the extra victory point. Use the Isolation Field on a territory where an opponent's bonus is fueling their strategy — removing Herbert Valley's Shipyard discount from a fleet-rushing player can cripple them. Save the Repulsor Field for the endgame to lock a territory where you have a one-colony lead, preventing opponents from contesting your control.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Dice Rolls
walkthrough.text: Unlike pure strategy games, Alien Frontiers has dice. The best players do not have a rigid plan — they have a flexible framework that adapts to what they roll. Before each turn, quickly scan all ten facilities and identify two or three possible placements for each die. Then find the combination that advances your goals the most. High dice are great for the Lunar Mine, Terraforming Station, and Solar Converter. Low doubles are perfect for the Orbital Market. Runs go to the Raiders' Outpost. Triples go to the Colony Constructor. Train yourself to see these patterns instantly and you will make better decisions under time pressure.

walkthrough.title: Multiplayer Dynamics
walkthrough.text: In a three or four-player game, the social dynamics of Alien Frontiers matter as much as the dice. The player in the lead will be targeted by everyone else. This means there is an advantage to staying just behind the leader until you are ready to sprint to the finish. Avoid drawing attention by placing colonies quietly through the Colonist Hub rather than flashy Colony Constructor plays. When you raid, target the leader so other players see you as an ally. Save your Alien Tech cards and resources for a burst finish — placing two colonies in consecutive turns is often enough to win before the table can react.

summary.title: The Shipyard Rush
bullets: Build 4th ship turn 1 (1 Fuel + 1 Ore), 5th ship turn 2-3 (2+2)
5 dice is the sweet spot — reliable access to combo facilities
6th ship (3+3) is expensive and only marginally better

Colonist Hub: fill tracks opponents are advancing on

Look for 2-card synergies that multiply options

entries.definition: Orbital facility with 8 docking ports. Accepts any die value. Gain Fuel equal to half the die value, rounded up. Die showing 1-2 = 1 Fuel, 3-4 = 2 Fuel, 5-6 = 3 Fuel.
Orbital facility with 5 docking ports. Each new ship docked must show a value equal to or higher than the highest die already docked. Gain 1 Ore per ship.

Orbital facility with 4 docking ports. Accepts any die value. Dock any ships to cycle (discard and redraw) the 3 face-up Alien Tech cards. Ships totaling 8 or more may claim 1 face-up Alien Tech card.

Orbital facility with 4 tracks, each having 3 docking ports and a 7-circle advancement track. Dock 1 ship per track to advance a colony. Launch the colony at the end of the track by paying 1 Fuel and 1 Ore.
Orbital facility. Dock 3 ships showing the same value and pay 3 Ore to immediately place 1 colony on any territory. Ships are not consumed.
Orbital facility. Dock 1 ship showing a 6 and pay 1 Fuel + 1 Ore to immediately place 1 colony. The docked ship is consumed (returned to general stock) and comes back at the start of your next turn.
Orbital facility. Dock 2 ships of equal value and pay Fuel + Ore to gain a new ship. Costs: 4th ship = 1F/1O, 5th = 2F/2O, 6th = 3F/3O. Maximum 6 ships per player.

Orbital facility. Dock 3 ships with sequential values (e.g. 1-2-3, 2-3-4). Steal 4 resource tokens from opponents (any combination) or 1 Alien Tech card from a single opponent. A higher sequence bumps a lower one.
Orbital facility. Dock 2 ships of equal value. Pay Fuel equal to the die value to gain 1 Ore. Can make multiple transactions per docking. Lower pairs give better exchange rates.
Holding area for ships that cannot legally dock at any orbital facility. Provides no benefit. Ships placed here at game start before the first turn.
Territory bonus: when you dock 2 or more ships at the Colonist Hub, advance one additional space on one of your tracks. Speeds up the slow Colonist Hub colonization path.
Territory bonus: Colony Constructor costs 1 less Ore (2 Ore instead of 3). Makes triple-dice colony placement significantly cheaper.
Territory bonus: you may purchase a Relic Ship (a clear/neutral die) for 1 Fuel + 1 Ore. The Relic Ship acts as an additional die on your turns.
Territory bonus: Orbital Market trades at a 1:1 Fuel-to-Ore ratio regardless of die value. Dramatically improves Fuel-to-Ore conversion efficiency.
Territory bonus: Shipyard builds cost 1 less Fuel and 1 less Ore. Makes fleet expansion much cheaper — 4th ship is free, 5th costs 1F/1O, 6th costs 2F/2O.
Territory bonus: gain 1 extra Fuel per ship docked at the Solar Converter. Significantly boosts Fuel income every turn.
Territory bonus: Alien Tech card Fuel-powered abilities cost 1 less Fuel. Makes repeated use of tech card abilities more affordable.
Territory bonus: your first ship docked at the Lunar Mine can be any value, ignoring the escalating requirement. Guarantees at least 1 Ore from the Lunar Mine each turn.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: add 1 to any die after rolling. Discard power: add 3 to any die after rolling (max 6). Useful for reaching specific values needed at facilities.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: look at top 3 cards of the Alien Tech deck. Discard power: place a ship at a facility even if all ports are full. Bypasses docking limits.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: adjust any 1 die by +1 or -1 after rolling. Discard power: set any 1 die to any value. Extremely versatile for hitting exact numbers.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: place a colony as if you had 1 additional colony in a territory for control purposes. Discard power: move 1 opponent's colony to a different territory.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: swap the positions of 2 of your ships on the board. Discard power: move 1 of your colonies from one territory to another.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: force an opponent to return 1 resource to the supply. Discard power: destroy 1 opponent's colony, returning it to their supply. Devastating disruption tool.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: reverse the value of 1 die (1 becomes 6, 2 becomes 5, etc.). Discard power: reverse the values of all your dice. Useful for converting high rolls to low or vice versa.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: gain 1 Fuel and 1 Ore from the supply. Discard power: gain 3 Fuel and 3 Ore from the supply (respecting the 8-token cap). Emergency resource injection.

Alien Tech card. Fuel power: after gathering your ships, keep 1 die at its previous value instead of rerolling. Discard power: keep all dice at their previous values. Guarantees repeating a good roll.
Alien Tech card. Fuel power: take another action with 1 ship that has already been placed. Discard power: take an entire additional turn after your current one. Game-winning in the endgame.
Field generator placed on a territory. The controller of that territory gains 1 additional victory point. Does not stack — only 1 Positron Field exists in the game.
Field generator placed on a territory. Nullifies the territory's bonus tile — the controller no longer receives the special ability. Used to shut down powerful territory synergies.
Field generator placed on a territory. Prevents any colonies from being added to or removed from the territory. Locks current control in place. Powerful endgame tool.

Orange resource token. Primary income from the Solar Converter. Used to launch colonies from the Colonist Hub, build ships at the Shipyard, terraform colonies, power Alien Tech abilities, and trade at the Orbital Market.

Gray resource token. Primary income from the Lunar Mine. Used to build colonies at the Colony Constructor, launch from the Colonist Hub, build ships at the Shipyard, and terraform colonies. Generally harder to acquire than Fuel.
Players may never hold more than 8 resource tokens (Fuel + Ore combined) at any time. Excess resources are not collected. Plan spending to avoid wasting potential income.

Dice in your player color that represent spaceships. Each player starts with 3 and can build up to 6 at the Shipyard. Ships are gathered, rolled, and assigned to orbital facilities each turn. More ships = more options.

Plastic or wooden token in your player color placed on Planet Maxwell's territories. Each player starts with 6 colonies. Each placed colony = 1 VP. The game ends when a player places their last colony.
The player with the most colonies in a territory controls it, gaining 1 VP and the territory's bonus ability. Ties mean no one controls the territory. Control is checked continuously.
A clear/neutral die available to the controller of Burroughs Desert for 1 Fuel + 1 Ore. Functions as an additional ship on your turns, increasing your dice pool.

A space at an orbital facility where a ship can be placed. Each facility has a limited number of ports. When all ports are full, no additional ships can dock there (unless an Alien Tech card overrides this).
At the Alien Artifact facility, discarding all 3 face-up Alien Tech cards and dealing 3 new ones from the deck. Available when docking any ships there, before claiming a card.
At the Raiders' Outpost, a player who docks a higher sequential set displaces a lower one. The bumped ships go to the Maintenance Bay and the bumped player loses their raid action.
Each turn has 3 phases: (1) Gather all your ships from the board, (2) Roll all your dice, (3) Assign all ships to orbital facilities. Ships that cannot dock go to the Maintenance Bay.

Victory points come from three sources: 1 VP per colony placed on the planet, 1 VP per territory controlled (most colonies), and 1 VP from the Positron Field generator if applicable. Game ends when a player places their last colony.
